coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical porous carbon synthesis by carbonized polymer dots-based sacrificial template for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jia
Liu, Chang
Jin, Yanzi
Chen, Jiucun
coredata.dc:description: The performance of carbon-based supercapacitors is primarily determined by their porous carbons with customizable structures. Targeted regulation of the porous' size and amount is a crucial but challenging research topic to pursue higher power density and energy density. Additional loading of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) on existing materials to optimize pore structures and modify pseudocapacitive groups is a feasible alternative. CPDs are treated as the desirable hard template, due to their high stability and controlled small-size to tailor reasonable pore distribution. Here, the hierarchical porous carbon was fabricated by activating the composite of CPDs and hyper-crosslinking polymer (HCP), which originated from the Friedel-Crafts reaction of small molecules. After the CPDs self-sacrificing template method based on the “excavation” of CPDs with small size (＜10 nm) by KOH, the optimal material owned abundant small mesopores (2 ∼ 10 nm) and large specific surface area (4241 m2·g−1). Thanks to the excellent structures, the optimized product yielded ultrahigh energy density at high power density (35.8 Wh·kg−1at 37497 W·kg−1) in 1 M TEATFB/PC. Thus, the CPDs-based pore-formation and regulation strategy is a potentially helpful alternative for fabricating high performance carbon-based supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical porous carbon synthesis by carbonized polymer dots-based sacrificial template for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing the transformation of CCPD@HCPM(CC).

The performance of carbon-based supercapacitors is primarily determined by their porous carbons with customizable structures. Targeted regulation of the porous' size and amount is a crucial but challenging research topic to pursue higher power density and energy density. Additional loading of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) on existing materials to optimize pore structures and modify pseudocapacitive groups is a feasible alternative. CPDs are treated as the desirable hard template, due to their high stability and controlled small-size to tailor reasonable pore distribution. Here, the hierarchical porous carbon was fabricated by activating the composite of CPDs and hyper-crosslinking polymer (HCP), which originated from the Friedel-Crafts reaction of small molecules. After the CPDs self-sacrificing template method based on the “excavation” of CPDs with small size (＜10 nm) by KOH, the optimal material owned abundant small mesopores (2 ∼ 10 nm) and large specific surface area (4241 m2·g−1). Thanks to the excellent structures, the optimized product yielded ultrahigh energy density at high power density (35.8 Wh·kg−1at 37497 W·kg−1) in 1 M TEATFB/PC. Thus, the CPDs-based pore-formation and regulation strategy is a potentially helpful alternative for fabricating high performance carbon-based supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of human society, global energy consumption has increased dramatically, and traditional energy sources can no longer meet the needs of industrial production and the residential life of human society. Therefore, it is crucial to develop sustainable and friendly energy technologies, such as lithium ion batteries, sodium ion batteries, fuel cells, metal-air batteries, and other energy storage technologies [1–7]. These energy storage technologies have been widely put into popular applications, such as intelligence appliances, new energy vehicles, drones, and aerospace [8,9], promoting people's low-carbon and environmentally friendly lives. However, both the development of clean energy and the upgrading of the manufacturing industries need to find suitable energy storage devices. Considering the safety, sustainability and large-scale use of new energy sources, batteries or supercapacitors are the first choice for energy storage. Currently, secondary batteries, such as lithium-ion batteries, have a low power density but a high energy density [10]. In addition, these batteries generate a lot of heat when they are rapidly charged and discharged at high current density, which can reduce the service life of the energy storage device [11]. Supercapacitors have been widely used in hybrid vehicles, industrial power units, and consumer electronics owing to their advantages of short charging time, high power density, and long lifetime [12,13]. There are two supercapacitors types: pseudocapacitor supercapacitor and the electrical double layer capacitor (EDLC) [14–18]. Among double the electrical double layer supercapacitors, carbon-based supercapacitors are more promising, due to the use of a class of porous carbon materials with large specific surface area (SSA) and rich pore characteristics [19]. Usually, the large number of micropores raises the SSA, which in turn can increase the charge storage [20,21]. As a fast channel for ion migration, the mesopores can significantly reduce ion resistance and enhance the power density of supercapacitors in organic electrolytes or ionic liquids [22]. However, an excessive amount of large mesopores are considered ineffective, or they not only reduce the specific surface area of the material but also destroy the physical integrity of the material. Therefore, it would be interesting to explore the preparation of hierarchical PCs with greatly enriched small mesopores [23,24]. Many methods have been proposed for tuning the pore and microstructure of PCs. These methods included nano-casting and the use of various hard or soft templates (e.g., nanosilicate, magnesium oxide or block copolymers) [25–27]. The PCs synthesized using hard templates were usually limited by the composition and size of the pore-forming material itself. At the same time, the PCs synthesized using soft templates were often associated with residual pore-forming agents, instability of the product properties and the introduction of impurities [28,29].

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with nano sizes and an abundance of functional groups are emerging members of the carbon family. They have been widely used in many fields, such as catalytic analysis, energy storage, and biotechnology [30–32]. At the same time, the CPDs have a certain degree of conductivity (sp 2 region). They are homologous in the bulk of the carbon material, which increases the specific surface area of the materials and reduces the system's internal resistance [33,34]. However, the application of CPDs in the field of supercapacitors is mainly as heteroatom dopants to increase pseudocapacitance as additives to enhance the electrochemical properties of the electrode materials [35–37]. There are few satisfactory reports on using CPDs as templates or pore-forming agents for the preparation of porous carbon. Herein, a CPD and hyper-crosslinking polymer (HCP) composite precursor (CPD@HCP(CC)) was prepared by Friedel-Crafts (F-C) alkylation of coal tar in the presence of a cross-linker (cyanuric chloride (CC)), followed by carbonization-activation to form the porous carbon materials (CCPD@HCPM(CC)) for supercapacitors application. The preparation process was depicted in Scheme 1 . Meanwhile, the specific pore-forming mechanism and the role of the cross-linker were investigated by setting up the contrast groups involving materials with similar structures but different cross-linker (1,3,5-trichlorobenzene (CB)) and pure polymer (without cross-linker). The electrochemical tests in organic systems have shown that carbon-based supercapacitors' performance was dependent on their structure. The CCPD@HCPM(CC) electrode showed prominent capacitance characteristics in 1 M TEATFB/PC organic electrolyte, with of 150.4 F·g−1 (at 1.0 A·g−1) and a capacity retention of 80.12 % after 5000 cycles. Its supercapacitors could achieve a high energy density of 47 Wh·kg−1 at a power density of 750 W·kg−1, and then maintained a high energy density of 35.8 Wh·kg−1 even at high power density of 37497 W·kg−1. The energy storage performance of CCPD@HCPM(CC) electrode was much higher than that of commercial activated carbon and showed great application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation process of the CPD@HCP(CC) precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike most reports on porous carbon electrode materials for supercapacitors [38,39], the precursor of the electrode materials in this work was a composite consisting of HCP frameworks and CPDs. CPD@HCP, as the precursor, has undergone three morphological changes, in the order of the 0-dimensional (0D) dot, 2D cluster and 3D chunk (see Supplementary material for detailed procedures). Coal tar, as the raw material, is a complex liquid rich in condensed aromatics and N- and O-containing aromatic derivatives (e.g., phenol, aniline, etc.), which can participate in the F-C reactions. It was worth noting that the elemental nitrogen content of coal tar was less than 1 % in Table S1 (Supplementary material). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the synthesis of carbon precursor involved an intermolecular F-C alkylation reaction between the aromatic rings in coal tar, with cyanuric chloride (CC) as the cross-linker, followed by the formation of intermediates with a loose structure. Meanwhile, the Cl atoms in the intermediate were attacked by AlCl3 to generate more carbonium ion intermediates which would react with the intramolecular aromatic rings to form a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon-like framework [24,40]. On account of the non-directiveness and high-reactivity of the F-C reaction at high temperature, the continuous carbonization eventually led to the formation and growth of CPDs. Moreover, the hyper-crosslinking polymerization proceeded rapidly and almost synchronized with the formation of CPDs. Within 5 min, CPD-5 with blue fluorescence appeared in the reaction mixture and tended to grow or cross-link with each other using CC as the cross-linker to form the CPD@HCP(CC)-5. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CPD-5 was shown in Fig. S1, which had apparent excitation-dependent emission properties. In the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CPD@HCP(CC)-5, no polymer matrix and lattice could be identified, as shown in Fig. 1B. And it found that CPD-5 was composed of particles with a diameter of 2.41 ± 0.1 nm. Within 30 min, the reaction mixture became viscous and consisted of CPD-30 and HCP (i.e., CPD@HCP(CC)-30). Nevertheless, the TEM image of CPD@HCP(CC)-30 (Fig. 1C and 1D) showed the cross-linked spherical nanoclusters with loose structures, and its electron diffraction of the selected region (SAED, Fig. 1C, inset) revealed the polycrystalline structure. The details of these aggregates were magnified in Fig. 1D, presenting the core–shell-like structures, in which dense CPDs agglomerated in the center to form the “core” while the loose HCP networks were distributed on the surface to form the “shell”. From the high-resolution (HR) TEM in Fig. 1E, it can be found that the CPDs possessed well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, while the HCP had no well-defined structures in their gaps. Apparently, the HCP mentioned above with non-compact structures acted as bridges between CPDs. Within 120 min, the reaction mixture (i.e., CPD@HCP(CC)-120) became so thick that the cross-linking polymerization reaction probably was no longer taking place, and would serve as the precursor for the hierarchical porous carbon. As shown in Fig. 1A and 1F, the clusters would be overlaid with newborn HCPs or mutually over-crosslinked, concurrently mixed with other free CPDs, and finally merged into the 3D mesh-like networks of CPD@HCP(CC)-120. The TEM and SAED images of CPD@HCP(CC)-120 (Fig. 1G and 1H) indicated that it possessed the crystalline type between amorphous and polycrystalline. Some CPDs were buried in the HCP matrix. As shown in Fig. 1I, some regions (within the white circles) had well-defined lattice edges with a spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane, which pointed to CPDs. In contrast, other shaded regions (within the blue circle) without crystallization were attributed to amorphous HCP substrates. Purely in terms of structure, the HCP matrix played the role of the framework with many unstable units derived from crosslinkers (e.g., triazine rings). These HCPs can be disrupted during activation, resulting in the tearing of the carbon chain segments, which not only promoted the formation of partial micropores but also contributed to the exposure or shedding of the CPDs with dense structures. Therefore, the HCP could be considered a soft template, while the CPD could be considered a hard template.

In order to facilitate “tearing” of the soft-templates and “stripping” of the hard-templates, the precursors, CPD@HCP(CC)-120 need to be treated with the suitable doses of KOH activator and the suitable temperature (see Supplementary material for detailed procedures). Finally, the CPD@HCP(CC) was activated at 1:1 of the weight ratio of KOH and the precursor under 800 °C. The obtained CCPD@HCPM(CC) sample exhibited the best characteristics in specific surface area, pore structure and electrochemical properties. As indicated by the field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images of Fig. 2 A-B and the TEM images of Fig. 1F-1I and Fig. 2C-F, the composite structures changed gradually from solid block-like to honeycomb-like and finally collapsed to broken pieces. In the magnified FESEM image of CCPD@HCPM(CC) (in Fig. 2B, right side), additional jagged and dense mesh-like 3D structures with interpenetrating channels were observed. Compared to CPD@HCP(CC)-120, the CCPD@HCPM(CC) possessed a thin layer graphene-like configuration with an uneven surface and more exposed CPDs. The well-defined diffraction points in the SAED images (Fig. 2D, inset) pointed to its higher degree of graphitization. The presence of rich CPDs increased the reaction sites or shuttle paths of ions, thus effectively improving the rate performance of supercapacitors [35,41]. More importantly, in the HRTEM images in Fig. 2E and 2F, abundant small mesopores of similar size to that of the CPDs (within the red circle) and clear boundaries between the CPDs and the carbonized matrixes could be distinguished. It indicated that the connections between the CPD and HCP in the precursor could be disrupted, resulting in the detachment of the CPDs and the cavities left behind. As such, in its precursors (Fig. 1H and 1I), the CPDs were encapsulated by the amorphous HCPs and cannot be clearly identified. While the CPDs in CCPD@HCPM(CC) were exposed, showing well-defined lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm. However, the carbonized substrates displayed different crystallinity with 0.34 nm spacing (Fig. 2F).

To further investigate the formation mechanism of CCPD@HCPM(CC), two contrast precursors and their corresponding activation products (see Supplementary material) were prepared. Based on our hypothesis (in Fig. S2), 1,3,5-trichlorobenzene (CB) with similar properties to CC can be used as an alternative crosslinker to react with coal tar to form the composite, which was named CPD@HCP(CB)-120. The critical difference between CPD@HCP(CC)-120 and CPD@HCP(CB)-120 was the composition of crosslinker. The former contained triazine rings in its HCP framework, while the latter had only aromatic compounds in its HCP framework, although both composites were composed of CPD and HCP. Meanwhile, without the addition of crosslinker, AlCl3 was used to initiate coal tar F-C reaction and self-polymerization to synthesize the pure hyper-crosslinking polymer (PHCP) [42,43]. The PHCP was further carbonized to obtain the CPHCPM sample. The subsequent analysis verified the relevance of the designs of the above materials. The SEM images in Fig. 2A-B and Fig. S3 showed similar transformation in the structure of the three precursors from bulk to honeycomb after activation. However, there were some differences in their TEM images (Fig. 2G-H). CPD@HCP(CB)-120, similar to CPD@HCP (CC)-120, had the 3D mesh structures with a small amount of embedded CPDs. On the other hand, CCPD@HCPM(CB), similar to CCPD@HCPM(CC), owned multi-microporous structures with highly crystalline multilayered graphene-like structure. From Fig. 2I-J, it can be found that PHCP-120 had a compact and smooth block structure, while CPHCPM presented the morphology of multilayer carbonized blocks. As such, the special formation mode of PHCP-120 resulted in a more homogeneous CPD-free polymer structure, depriving the tendency of an etching by KOH and thus affecting the structure of CPHCPM.

The structural characteristics of all samples were explored through Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S4, all samples possessed three Raman peaks near 1400, 1610, and 2750 cm−1, corresponding to the D, G, and 2D peaks, respectively. The three activated products exhibited similar crystallinity above that of their corresponding precursors. However, the three activated products owned smoother curves without spurious peaks and smaller values of ID/IG compared to their precursors, which pointed to the more regular and complete sp2 -C conjugation structure and support to realize a good conductivity and facilitate the fast charge transportation [44,45]. Comparing the peak intensity ratios of ID/IG of the porous carbon prepared by the other two methods, it can be found that the peak intensity ratios of ID/IG of the precursor prepared by using cyanuric chloride as the crosslinker decreases less after high temperature activation, which indicated that the use of cyanuric chloride could slow down the decrease in the degree of defects in CCPD@HCPM(CC). It's also shown that the exposure of CPDs in the material increased the defects of porous carbon materials to some extent. Surprisingly, the graphitization of CCPD@HCPM(CB) material was higher than that of CPD@HCP(CB)-120, which meant that the introduction of 1,3,5-trichlorobenzene suppressed the defects caused by the exposure of CPDs in CCPD@HCPM(CB), to affect the electrochemical performance of porous materials. Meanwhile, the CPHCPM prepared without the addition of crosslinker showed less defect reduction, which was more likely due to the disorderly etching of KOH.

Some variations could be distinguished by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Figs. S5–S8 and Table S2). CPD@HCP(CC)-120 and CPD@HCP(CB)-120 had almost identical C, N, O and Cl contents, while PHCP-120 had no Cl element. Due to the embedding of the CPDs, the HR-XPS C1s peak components of CPD@HCP(CC)-120 and CPD@HCP(CB)-120 displayed the same characteristics as that of CPDs, both of which be subdivided into five peak components, corresponding to the CC/CC, CN, C–Cl, CN/CO, and CO species, respectively (Table S3). However, the C1s spectrum of PHCP-120 exhibited similar characteristics as that of other traditional carbon-based precursors [46,47]. It could be were subdivided into six peak components, corresponding to sp2 -C, sp3 -C, CN/CO, CO, O–CO, and π → π*, respectively (Table S4). The HR-XPS N1s peak of CPD@HCP(CC)-120 can be subdivided into the peak components for N in C3N3 and graphitic N, while that of CPD@HCP(CB)-120 and PHCP-120 were subdivided into the peak components of amino N and graphitic N. After activation, the XPS and HR-XPS results of the three products (Fig. S9 and Table S5) indicated a high degree of carbonization. The C contents were higher than 88 %, the N contents were less than 0.91 %, and the contents of sp2 -C associated with conjugate structures were higher than 64 %. Meanwhile, the C1s split peaks of CCPD@HCPM(CC) and CCPD@HCPM(CB) were distinctly different from that of their precursors, owing to the shedding of CPDs and the removal of heteroatoms, as well as the appearances of sp2 -C and π → π* peaks. The O1s HR-XPS spectra of CCPD@HCPM(CC) and CCPD@HCPM(CB) showed more –OH than OC groups, indicating that the O atoms originated from the residual OH− after molten KOH etching rather than from its own oxidation. The energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis in Fig. S10 verified the above points, visualizing the elemental distribution of the material before or after activation, from which the lack of N- and Cl- doping in the activated product could be observed, implying their electrochemical properties were not affected by the heteroatoms.

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were performed to investigate the specific surface area and pore size distribution of these three cross-linked products before and after the activation of pore formation, as shown in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12. Three cross-linked precursors all showed typical type III isotherms with an obvious hysteresis loop of H3-type at a relative pressure (P/P0) = 0.8–1.0 between the adsorption/desorption curves, suggesting a dominant characteristic of the macroporous materials. We analyzed the differences in their specific surface area, pore volume, and the percentage of pore size distribution from 2 nm to 300 nm, as shown in Fig. 2K-O and Table S6. As a whole, the pore distribution ratio of macropores (>50 nm) in the three precursors was relatively high (in Fig. 2K and Fig. S12A), and their microporous pore volumes were relatively low (in Fig. 2L and 2 M), which was consistent with the results of the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms. CCPD@HCPM(CC) sample's nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (in Fig. S9) exhibited the classical I/IV type characteristics. The CCPD@HCPM(CC) material owned a total specific surface area (SBET) of 4241 m2·g−1, the total pore volume (Vtotal) of 2.50 cm3·g−1, a microporous specific surface area (Smicro) of 75 %, and a relatively small average pore size of 2.14 nm, which stood out from other samples. The results showed that CCPD@HCPM(CC) was a kind of porous carbon material with predominantly microporous and hierarchic porous characteristics. On the one hand this was due to the CPDs-based pore formation method. On the other hand, the large number of self-generated CPDs exposed on the surface of CCPD@HCPM(CC) material (see Fig. 2E-F) helped increase the surface area of the electrode material and thus charge adsorption. Moreover, unlike most reported porous carbons, its pore size distribution (PSD) curve was smooth in the region above 10 nm and undulating in the region smaller than 10 nm. Small-mesopores (with size of 2 to 10 nm) in CCPD@HCPM(CC) accounted for more than 92 % of the total number of meso- and macro-pores, indicating that the shedding of CPD was the most critical pathway for the formation of mesopores in our materials (see Fig. 2K, Fig. S12B and Table S7). Thus, most of the pores were small micro- and meso-pores that matched the electrolyte radius [48]. The double electric layer capacitors (EDLCs) relied on rich micropores to store charges. The above small mesopores can facilitate the entry of electrolytes into the micropores to promote the electrostatic separation of ions from the electrode surface, increasing the power density of electrode materials [49]. On the contrary, the CPHCPM had the smoothest structure with SBET, Smicro and Vtotal values of 1721 m2·g−1, 1500 m2·g−1 and 0.84 cm3·g−1, respectively, and an undulating PSD curve from 2 to 100 nm. It was much lower than the specific surface area and pore volume of CCPD@HCPM (CC) material. The small mesopores in CPHCPM accounted for 43 % of the total pore volume number of meso- and macro-pores. In comparison, the pores with a size larger than 50 nm in CPHCPM accounted for 44 %, suggesting that the formation of pores loses directionality in the absence of CPDs. As for the products activated by the complexes composed of CPD and HCP, the SBET, Smicro, and Vtotal values of CCPD@HCPM(CB) were 1878 m2·g−1, 1547 m2·g−1 and 1.02 cm3·g−1, respectively, with smoother PSD curves from 2 to 100 nm. The Smicro ratio of the CPHCPM and CCPD@HCPM(CB) materials accounted for 87.2 % and 82.4 %, respectively, which were higher than that of the CCPD@HCPM(CC). Compared to CPHCPM, the CCPD@HCPM(CB) had more small mesopores, indicating that the shedding of CPDs remained the most relevant approach to pore formation. It can also be seen that the structural parameters of CCPD@HCPM(CB) were closer to those of CPHCPM rather than those of CCPD@HCPM(CC) under the same activation process. Meanwhile, the pore formation in CPD@HCP(CB) and PHCP was much more difficult than that in CPD@HCP(CC). Therefore, the advantages of the above pore formation process in CCPD@HCPM(CC) were mainly associated with the ultra-high specific surface area and unique pore distribution, which in turn affected the performance of the products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The perforation mechanism of porous carbons
ce:section.ce:para: Different composition and structure of the precursors gave rise to different activation pathways, which affected the products' performance. The benzene rings derived from CB were considered the polymer cross-linking unit in CPD@HCP(CB)-120. Its structural stability was close to that of aromatic compound derived from coal tar on PHCP-120 rather than the triazine ring in CPD@HCP(CC)-120. The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis results of the three precursors can be used to reveal the hypothesis, as described in Fig. S13. For CPD@HCP(CB)-120 and PHCP-120, there was no significant difference in their TG curves when the temperature was below 600 °C. The rate of weightlessness of the former was obviously greater than that of the latter when the temperature was above 600 °C, indicating that the relatively loose composite structure of CPD@HCP(CB)-120 had reduced its thermal stability. As for CPD@HCP(CC)-120, it had a high rate of weightlessness from 400 to 620 °C, and the derivative of thermogravimetry (DTG) analysis curve had a peak at 505 °C, both of which corresponded to the thermal decomposition temperature of triazine [50]. Moreover, the retained mass ratio of CPD@HCP(CC)-120 at 800 °C was 10.56 %, which was much lower than that of the other two, indicating again that the presence of triazine rings was a key factor contributing to the thermodynamic instability.

The chemical stability of the three precursors was further measured by DFT calculations of the reaction energy (RE, i.e., binding energy) between the cross-linking units and KOH (OH−), which were performed using Gaussian16 at the M06-2X/6–311 + g (d, p) level [51,52]. According to the literature [53,54], the molten KOH (OH−) preferentially attacked the highly active sites and reacted rapidly with unsaturated carbon (e.g., =CH2 and ≡CH) to produce H2, K2CO3 and K2O, among others. Subsequently, driven by high temperature, K2CO3 and K2O continued to corrode the C or N elements around them, eventually tearing the carbon layers. Based on the above considerations, we built six simplified models. The CP-1 model in Fig. 3 C represented the cross-linking structure on CPD@HCP(CC)-120, in which the 1-, 3- and 5-substitution sites of the triazine ring were replaced by benzene rings representing the HCP framework. The C elements at these positions were vulnerable to attack by OH− to form the CP-2 model because they were in the N-heteroarenes unsaturated system. The BP-1 model in Fig. 3D represented the cross-linking structure on CPD@HCP(CB)-120, where the unsubstituted sites on the central benzene ring could easily combine with OH− due to the lack of spatial resistance to form the BP-2 model. PHCP-120 had a dense structure with a homogeneous composition, so it can be represented by the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon-like NP-1 model, whose edges react with OH– to form the NP-2 model in Fig. 3E. Comparing the Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG) of the three pairs of models in Fig. 3C-E and Table S8, it was found that the reaction energy (RE) from CP-1 to CP-2, BP-1 to BP-2, and NP-1 to NP-2 were −1.81, −1.21, and −1.13 eV, respectively, corresponding to the gradual increase in the difficulty of the reactions. Thus, the use of crosslinking agent enhanced the reactivity of precursors, which was effectively improved by CC rather than CB. Driven by high-temperature, KOH acted as “shovels” and attacked the unstable structures of the HCP in the precursors, resulting in the tearing of the carbon film and the falling-off of the CPDs, as shown in Fig. 3A. As for PHCP-120, as shown in Fig. 3B, KOH would randomly degrade the pure polymer of uniform structure and non-directionally etch their surface to generate more macropores.

Owing to the lack of doping effect by the heteroatoms in electrode materials, the electrochemical performance of the EDLCs was closely related to the structures of electrode materials [55,56]. The EDLCs was assembled to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the optimal sample and two contrast samples in organic electrolyte. In 1 M TEATFB/PC electrolyte, the optimal sample (CCPD@HCPM(CC)) exhibited the best characteristics under 0 ∼ 3 V. In its cyclic voltammetry (CV) response plots (Fig. 4 A-4B), the similar quasi-rectangles along the current–potential axis was not distorted at scan rates of 5 ∼ 500 mV·s−1, which mirrored the ideal capacitive behavior. Correspondingly, its galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves (Fig. 4C-4D) had an isosceles triangle shape and remained stable from 0.2 to 50 A·g−1, implying that the sample’s specific surface promoted the effective adsorption and desorption of ions or charges [57]. According to the calculation equation 1 (see Supplementary material), it maintained a specific capacitance (C cell) of 150.4F·g−1 at 1.0 A·g−1 and a high C cell of 114.6F·g−1 at 50 A·g−1, indicating excellent rate performance at high currents. The Nyquist plot of the CCPD@HCPM(CC) was shown in Fig. 4E. Its low diffusion distribution resistance (Zw), charge transfer resistance (Rct) and internal resistance (Rs) were 1.37, 2.77, and 1.35 Ω, respectively, indicating the electrolyte ions interacted quickly with the interface. Meanwhile, the charges in CCPD@HCPM(CC) electrode were transferred efficiently during the process of charging/discharging. The CV curve of CCPD@HCPM(CB) displayed slight deformation at scan rates of 5 ∼ 500 mV·s−1 (Fig. S14A), while that of CPHCPM showed severe polarization (Fig. S15A). Meanwhile, in comparison to two contrast samples (Fig. 4F-G and Table S9), CCPD@HCPM(CC) exhibited a standard rectangle in CV plot with the maximal integral area at scan rate of 50 mV·s−1, and owned higher C cell value of 150.4F·g−1 at 1.0 A·g−1 (98.9F·g−1 for CCPD@HCPM(CB) and 88.3F·g−1 for CPHCPM). This result occurs due to the higher adsorption specific surface area of CCPD@HCPM(CC). In addition, the exposure of CPDs increased the presence of defects in CPD@HCPM(CC) to some extent. As a result, the specific capacitance value of CPD@HCPM(CC) electrode was higher than that of CPD@HCPM(CB) electrode at the same current density, which was consistent with the results of the Raman analysis. Meanwhile, the lower specific capacitance value of CPHCPM electrodes was more likely due to the lower specific surface area of CPHCPM materials. Also, reflected by the rate performance in Fig. S14B, S15B, and Fig. 4I, the Ccell of CCPD@HCPM(CC) retained the value of 114.6F g−1 at 50 A·g−1, while that of CCPD@HCPM(CB) and CPHCPM dropped sharply to 43.2F·g−1 and 13.3F·g−1, respectively. It benefited from the fact that CCPD@HCPM(CC) had more abundant small mesopores released from the CPDs after they were exposed or exfoliated from the carbon matrix. Fig. S16 showed the complete voltage distributions of three electrodes during the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) test at 0.2 A·g−1. According to Equation 2 (see Supplementary material), the values of ion diffusion coefficient (D) of CCPD@HCPM(CC) were in the range of 1.76 × 10−8 to 1.49 × 10−14 cm2 s−1 during the charge/discharge process. While the values of ion diffusion coefficient of CCPD@HCPM(CB) were in the range of 1.49 × 10−8 to 6.15 × 10−14 cm2 s−1, the values of ion diffusion coefficient of CPHCPM were in the range of 1.64 × 10−8 to 5.96 × 10−14 cm2 s−1. Therefore, the CCPD@HCPM(CC) had a higher ion diffusion coefficient than that of two comparison samples to facilitate the electrochemical kinetic process. As shown in the Nyquist plot of Fig. 4H and Table S10, CCPD@HCPM(CC) had a smaller Zw value than the other two samples (4.97 Ω for CCPD@HCPM(CB) and 6.35 Ω for CPHCPM), indicating that the rate of ion migration was the critical factor affecting their EDLCs performances, which also depended on the structure with proper pore distribution. The Nyquist plots also reflected that CCPD@HCPM(CC) had a reasonable interface and spatial structure suitable for accelerating ion diffusion and charge transport conversion.

Except for the capacitance and rate performance, the cycle stability was also a key to evaluate the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors [58]. The capacity retention of the CCPD@HCPM(CC) electrode remained at 80.12 %, and its coulombic efficiency was 99.78 % after 5000 cycles (Fig. 4J). These values were higher than those of the contrast samples (Figs. S17 and S18). Meanwhile, comparing the Nyquist plots of the three materials before and after 5000 cycles, it was found that the characteristics of CCPD@HCPM(CC) were the same as the Nyquist plots before 5000 cycles, except for a slight decrease in the ion diffusion slope in the low-frequency region (Fig. S19A). Unexpectedly, the charge transfer impedance and ion diffusion slope showed a severe decrease in both comparison samples (Fig. S19B, S19C). After 5000 cycles, the FESEM images observations of the three electrodes showed that both CCPD@HCPM(CB) and CPHCPM electrode showed significant cracking and binder detachment from the active material (Fig. S20). However, no similar phenomenon was found in the CCPD@HCPM(CC) electrode, and the active electrode material adhered to the collector (Al foil) very firmly. It fully indicated that the stability of CCPD@HCPM(CC) material was much higher than that of CCPD@HCPM(CB) and CPHCPM materials after several charge/discharge cycles at high current density. To better highlight the performance advantages of the optimal sample, the commercial activated carbon (YP-50F) was also performed electrochemical tests in 1 M TEATFB/PC electrolyte. The capacity retention of the YP-50F electrode can remain at 82.04 % with 99.89 % coulombic efficiency after 5000 cycles (in Fig. S21). The Ragone plots of samples in supercapacitors were shown in Fig. 4K [42,43,59–63]. The YP-50F electrode owned the lower energy density of 27.97 Wh·kg−1 at a power density of 750 W·kg−1, while our optimal sample reached 47 Wh·kg−1 at 750 W·kg−1 and even can maintain 35.8 Wh·kg−1 at 37497 W·kg−1, which also surpassed those of the two contrast samples (Fig. S22), according to the calculation equation 3 and 4 (see Supplementary material). The performances of the optimal sample (CCPD@HCPM(CC)) electrode had exceeded that of the most carbon-based porous electrode materials reported (Table S11). Finally, the device of the optimal sample can light up the LED (nominal voltage 12 V) for 3 min (Fig. 4L).

CPD-based aromatic polymer composites were synthesized and used as precursors to form porous carbon for supercapacitor applications. We focused on the formation process of the precursors, its pore-formation mechanism, and the role of cross-linking unit (triazine ring) in the activation process. CPDs were first synthesized by F-C alkylation between the cross-linker and coal tar and then acted as the reaction core to produce the unique composite structure, with CPD embedded in the HCP framework. Two contrast group experiments and related theoretical calculations further illustrated that KOH preferentially attacked the unstable units on the HCP framework, leading to the exposure or “release” of the small-sized CPD. Thanks to the unique approach of sacrificing the HCP and stripping the CPDs, the optimal sample (CCPD@HCPM(CC)) had a large specific surface area, suitable pore distribution, and rich small pores of adjustable sizes of 2 ∼ 10 nm, making it potentially useful in the field of supercapacitor fabrication.
The related data are shown in the Supplementary materials .
